speaker: interviewer
text: We're on tape eight with Mrs. Rachel Warzauer Schlufman, August 29, 1995. Mrs. Schlufman, you were telling me about the liberation. Right after the Russians liberated you, you and your sister were crossing all these dead bodies. Where did you go right after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there. The Red Cross gave us like a barrack, a military barrack. They gave us like white, off-white linen dresses. They took off our prison dresses. They sprayed us with powder, and we stayed there for three days. And, and they gave us very light food, like l-- rice, liquid rice for three days and some white bread. But after a few days, we didn't see the Red Cross. They ran away because it was rumored that the Germans are coming back. Now, I couldn't walk. My feet were swollen and bruised, but we stayed another two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my sister insisted, how bad I feel, we have to try to walk, to walk and see where we can stay together because I looked around. It was-- I didn't see a soul. We were frightened. Maybe Germans will come. And they will come to kill us. Maybe some German's hiding in the woods. Who knows? It was always the fear, you know, if you don't see people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we started to walk. While we walked, we did see another three survivors. They were in a Leipzig camp, and I knew them because we worked together in the same, same facility. And we worked again in five in a row, like we worked in the camp. We walked and walked and every-- and in the-- we walked the whole day. We stopped. We sat. We, we-- food, we were searching in the basements. But the German people, the German houses-- they left the houses. They were empty. Like I mentioned before, they went to all run away to the American side. So all the food and all the goods-- they left a lot of clothes and things. The best they took with themselves, but for us, this was the best, whatever they left. So we, we find some dresses. We find some shoes, even if they didn't fit, well, we put them on. And we started to look normal, a little normal again. We found cans of food, and we tried to open them, and we ate. We find potatoes and carrots and the pots and the pans in the kitchen. We started, you know, to cook. But the hassle started, that, and the Russian army was moving back, moving towards Berlin. The war wasn't s-- the war was still on. We were liberated, but the war wasn't over. They were still fighting. It took another month for them, you know, to, to, to, to fight in Berlin, you know, to go and to take Berlin. But all these little towns in Germany were taking-- were liberated by the Americans and by the English and by-- you know, like, I don't have to explain you. You perhaps know the history.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I-- it was a big hassle with the Russians. What can I tell you? We were young girls. We were-- we were humiliated every night. We were so-- we were tired from walking all day long, coming into the house and wanting to take off the dress and the shoes. And they had beds with the pillows, what they left in there-- and sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they, they got into the houses, and they were fighting with these survivors, with me. I'm not talking about somebody else. I have my own experience fighting almost every night with Russian soldiers not to be raped. I came across-- which it's a beautiful story-- a story which opened my mind to remind me to be what I am, to be a Jew. I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] fighting every night. It was just terrible-- begging them, crying for them. They didn't care. They just-- they-- the answer was that they've-- they're going to Berlin, and they don't know if they will come back alive. And they need woman. They don't have German women. That they-- they settled for the survivors.

speaker: interviewer
text: If we told them we're Polish, wasn't good. We t-- Jews, we were afraid to say, you know? We were afraid to tell that we are Jewish. So we told them we are Polish. It was even worse. Perhaps we would have told them that Jews, will be better. You know?

speaker: interviewee
text: One time, it was without even thinking that we could get away that a J-- a soldier came in with a-- with anoth-- two soldiers came in, Russian soldiers. They knock-- they just ripped our door apart, and they slipped into our-- to-- to the house where we slept at night.

speaker: interviewer
text: One was a Mongol. A Mongol is a Russian with the big eyes, with the big noses, the short people, you know? The Mongols, they are S-- S-- Siberia, but they live in these-- you know, this is a sect like that. And we called them Mon-- Mongols. But they're Russians. The other one looked normal to me, but somehow, I looked at his face, his features. I-- it didn't look to me like he's a Russian, and he didn't look like a Gentile.

speaker: interviewee
text: He did what I says-- it's just a miracle. Everything's a miracle. He says, I give you five minutes to get dressed. They have to take us to, to the-- to a place where they stayed, in their quarters-- you know, a lot of Russians. Five minutes. We started to scream. We started to cry. The Mongol was so mean. He gave a-- with that rifle, he says, if you-- if you're not going to stop screaming-- not to me. The other girls screamed more than I. I used more my mind-- my, my brains-- what to do, how to get out from that situation. Screaming didn't help you too much because you made them more mad and, and annoyed them more. But look for some-- what to do. I said, stop it, to the girls. I said, Sarah-- the other one was Dora-- I say, please, girls, stop. Let's, let's think about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said it in Yiddish. It slipped out in Yiddish to my sister Tobcha. I says, [YIDDISH]. He looks to be a Jew. My sister looks at me. How could you say that? He's going to take the rifle and kill you right away on the place. How could you risk your life and say that? She's telling-- she's telling me in Yiddish, you know? This Jewish soldier who would never believe that he's a Jewish soldier heard me saying, [YIDDISH] Jewish. His-- his face shown with a smile, and he pointed his finger to me with a smile. And he says, what did you say to your sister, to this la-- to this girl? I says-- when he asked me that with a smile, I had a warm feeling already. I says, you know what I said? I said it in Yiddish that you look to be our own people, our own kind. He says, you want to tell me that [YIDDISH], that you are Jewish girls? I says, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we both embraced each other with tears, and we were so happy. He kissed us. He says, you know, it's just a miracle that Rachela mentioned in Yiddish. Otherwise, you five girls of you, all of you, would be raped by many, many Russian soldiers. You would be crippled tonight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name by any chance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nah, no. And then, he told me-- and then he told me-- he told his story, that he's a Polish Jew. He went away to Russia. They took him to the army. He took him to the army, and here he was. They sent him out to get women. When he spotted us, and his heart woke up when he heard the Jewish word. When I heard that, when I witnessed this, when this Jewish soldier, he says, [YIDDISH], I'm so proud to find you Jewish girls. And I hope you'll be lucky to go back to Poland, and you find your family. I don't have hope, he says. And he told us that [INAUDIBLE] killed his brother before he, he went away to Russia, and the rest of him sis-- all kind of bad stories. OK. And we picked ourselves up next morning, and we walked and walked until we find a train, you know, a station. The Russians were telling us that a train is going to Lodz. From there, we could get in. We could get on and, and go, you know, to our destination. Well, we were very lucky. When we came to that train station, and we asked them if the train will take us to Lodz, he says, yes. You have to wait late in the evening. That train will leave. It was a wagon train, not a regular train, a train like a business of a wagons, you know-- open wagons, not closed wagons. And all five of us, we got in, and it took us a couple of days. We reached the city of Lodz. We come into the station. Of course, like I mentioned, nobody was waiting for us. No, no big deal survivors came after such a harrow. We sit-- we're sitting on the train station and waiting a whole night. We came in the evening. Perhaps we go on the street. We spot some survivors. Maybe they can tell us where to go or where to turn.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the morning, we slept on that cement floor a whole night. And the little bundles what we had, everyone had a little bundles which we took from the German basements, what we had, the shoes with the dresses, whatever we got. And a man came over to us and ask us. And he says, are you survivors? We proudly said, yes, we are. Well, welcome. So where is this? What is the situation? Where do we stand? He says, don't worry. Don't worry. We will take care of you. You come. I'll take you to the committee. Over there, you will get support. They gave us some money, and they gave us a room [POLISH] five in Lodz. Over there, we lived five girls together, and it didn't take us long. Food, we had. A few dollars, they gave us. In a short two weeks, we looked so nice. We went-- we jumped back to ourselves. We were back to our normal life, you know? The youth sprang back to us. But our heart and our feelings were gone. But the health came back, the health. The feelings, you know? And to-- Where did you go from Lodz?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Lodz, my husband came. These three girls with whom we stayed--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where you met your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were cousins of my husband. He searched for them, and he find them through the committee. He took the address. And here on an early morning, I see a nice young gentlemen. Knocks at the door, and he came in. And he ask about these three girls. I says, OK, sit down, and wait for them. I didn't pay too much attention. I didn't think about marriage. I, I, I, I wasn't-- my mind wasn't yet for marriage, I think. I just wanted to see the world, the sunshine. He looked at me a few times. I didn't know why he's staring at me like that. But after he left, he ask his cousins, and he wanted to find out if I get along with my sister and we don't fight and if I'm from a good background and if I'm a decent girl, if I don't run around in the evening, if I don't-- you know, so and so and so. He took all the information from his cousins, and I'm sure they gave him-- they told him all about, which was true. And a week later, he came. And straight, he walked over to me and he says, Rachela. He likes me. I appeal to him. He saw many girls after he survived. He think I am the only one. But after two weeks, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he walked-- he says to me, listen, my dear Rachela. I cannot promise you health. I cannot promise you richness. This lays in God's hands, but love, forever. We took-- he took me around and with that, we got engaged. And a few weeks later, we got married. That means in six weeks' time, we both got married. And I'm grateful and I'm happy to be together and live with my husband. We should only live together. I have two wonderful sons.

speaker: interviewer
text: We traveled. We hustled. Three months later after we got married, we traveled to Wald-- Waldenburg, to Ulm. We went from place to place with the Hagganah took us out from the Russian-- from Lodz, you know? We went away. We didn't want to stay with the Russians. We wanted to be and the-- with the Americans on the American side. Many, many survivors did the same thing. They followed the same step. Once [INAUDIBLE] followed the other.

speaker: interviewee
text: Until we reached Augsburg because we find out that Herb had a brother who immigr-- who came from also from Germany, you know, a few weeks, a few months, a few weeks later than we. And he established already. He found an apartment in Augsburg and his tante, his aunt and uncle, with their son. So I felt that I have a little family through him. And me and Tobcha, of course, we never separate. I went with Tobcha. And of course, Tobcha remarried in Lodz with-- she find her right man, Joel, whom I brought him to her to-- he was-- he survived the partisans. And he lost his wife with a six-month-old baby. It's a long story to tell, but I have to make it in short. And he-- when I met him in the committee, I met him like, like a brother, he came to me. He was a neighbor of ours from Wachock. And when I told him Tobcha survived, he was so excited. And of course, I took him home-- took him to [POLISH] five and Tobcha met him. It didn't take long. Even Tobcha promised me-- she told me she will never get married again. She didn't-- she was too injured.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I told her, you're so young, and why not? There are many young girls in your age survived. So whatever you lost, build a-- build a new life. And she listened to me. And Joel and Tobcha got married. And the day when they took [HEBREW] just with a few people, we got engaged with the words what Herb told me. And I told Tobcha, and she says, he's a wonderful man. And I-- his family is wonderful. I got to know his family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was very happy that he is brought up from a nice home, which she got to know him through the cousins, the background, which was very important to my sister. And, and then, we both two couples got married. Tobcha became pregnant. Three months later, I became-- no, six-- two months later, I became pregnant. That means three months after I got married, I became pregnant. I was married in January 1946-- January 2, 1946. And I came to Augsburg, and we lived with the family, with his brother. With the aunt, we visited. Of course, Tobcha got an apartment from the committee not far away. I had one room apartment but very happy-- very happy as long as I had Herb, my husband, together and my sister. And then, I heard from Sam. Of course, I started to find out from the committee, from the Jewish committee. They had all the listings that Sam survived, you know, and he's in England. And he-- they took 500 youngsters, and he was among them. And that was another story, which it's very interesting story.

speaker: interviewer
text: While I was in Lodz, I heard Sam survived. He's in Feldafing. And somebody told me that he's in Feldafing, and I was engaged already to, to Herb. I picked myself up and traveled with a train to Feldafing from Lodz, you know? That was such a risky trip to take, to go through the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my sister got me with a-- with a-- with a cruise, with a few people together-- I shouldn't be on myself-- with reliable people, with older people, you know, what she knew. And she says, take care of my sister. She wants to find her brother, and she wants to bring him back. I say, I want my brother to be on my wedding because I should have told you that before.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I cannot miss because that's a beautiful story. I said to Tobcha, I got to bring Shmulek back. I got to be together with Shmulek. He's the only brother what I have. She says, go ahead and do it. So she went and hustled. She got some elderly men. She knew it from [INAUDIBLE] or someplace else. And I traveled with them, hustling for three days, coming to Feldafing. Herb gave me the permission. I was engaged. He was sure for me I'm not going to find another boy so he says, I'm going back to my aunt. He went back to his aunt, to his brother who lived in, in Bra-- Bratislava. And I says, OK, [? I-- I ?] will take me a week, I will-- you know, three days back, three days you know, going. So in a week, I will be back, but I've got to find my brother. Come to Feldafing. The man took me with whom I traveled, and he says, Rachel, here's, here's the camp, the DP camp. I walk into this DP camp, and I come into the office. I was so tired. I sat down, and I had a little-- a little luggage-- a little luggage just to change my, my underwear, my nightgown, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I says-- he says, who are you? I says, I am Rachel Warszauer. I came to look for my brother, Sam War-- Sam Warzauer, Shmulek Warzauer. He's supposed to be in this camp. He looks at me. He takes out a picture. He says, this is your brother? I says, yes. Yes, where is he? In what bunk? He says, I'm sorry to tell you. It's bad news, and it's good news. What is the good news, I told him. God forbid if he's not in a hospital. What is the good news? Tell me the good news. I don't want to hear bad news anymore. He says, the good news is he went to England with a group of survivors in his age. The English people brought over, you know, about 500 of them, and he was among them. He left yesterday with the plane. You just missed one day. My god, how I took a such a long trip to come and to get my little Shmulek. Now you took him away? I says to him, why did you send him away? He says, no, Shmulek, this-- he knew that you survived, but he was looking for opportunity. He didn't want to stay in Germany in that DP camp. In England, he will have an opportunity to work himself up, to go back to school as a youngster with so many. And they talked him in. And he says, my sisters will find me, and I'm leaving to England. And you know how youngsters are. And he left. I cried and I cried. I says, when will I stop crying? When will be the day when I will stop crying? I says, now-- I says, OK. He says, don't worry, Miss Warszauer. Your brother is in good hands. A nice future is waiting for him in England. You will see. You're going to have a lots of nachas from your brother. You will be a pride. You will have pride from-- you will take pride from your brother. He is a-- he's a wonderful youngster. I met him yesterday. And I says, OK. You went back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], I told them. Thank you for the information. And I started to look around for a few people who going back because I was afraid to travel back by myself. And I hustled back with these two men, with a few men back to Lodz. And again, I was in the hands of Herb. And that's the story. We went. Here, I was in Augsburg with his family. Every story-- every story of mine has something different to tell and by coincident, it's sad and it's nice. Is it good, or it's bad, whatever. It's a-- it's a part of our life. And, and then I-- in nin-- I came to Augsburg. Two years later, my brother Shmulek came to visit us. That was the most beautiful reunion--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where? In Augsburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Augsburg. My son was at that time a baby, and that was such a thrilling coincident. My brother came, my Shmulek. I couldn't recognize him. He was at that time 20. He was 19. I was shocked that he was shaving. I look-- it looked so weird to me that Shmulek was shaving. I never thought that-- I looked at his face. It was like a girlish face when he was in concentration, a baby face. Here, he was shaving.

speaker: interviewer
text: I looked at him, and I says, oh, is this Shmulek? He survived, such a wonderful brother. I have one brother, at least one brother. I meant to myself, and I talked to myself at least  one Kaddish prayer after my father survived, [SOB] to remember to say Kaddish after them. And after years later, I went-- my sons-- we came years later, you know? After three years staying in Germany, my sister Tobcha decided to be the first pioneer to go to Israel with Joel. She had two little children. Shlomo was one and Chanala, her little girl, was six months old. She went to Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: We thought we'd never survive-- we'd never separate. But Tobcha, as the oldest, said to me, look, Rachela, we go first. We are the pioneers. Joel, you know, he was a Miz-- from the Mizrahi. For h-- for him, Israel was the first step to-- after he survived, to live to see Israel. There was-- this was their wish-- ours, too. We didn't think to come to America. We all thought to go to Israel. But after receiving letters from my sister and warning us not to come because the situation in 1948 was unb-- it was unbearable. It was very hard, especially for survivors, in Israel. And she said, stay there in America. And if you have-- if you-- if you have a possibility to go to another country, like America, like many people do, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: So she convinced you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I says, but how can we separate? She says--

speaker: interviewer
text: She convinced you to go from Augsburg to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: So, yes. So she says, look, Rachela, go to America because when you come to Israel, Herb is young. He's 20-- you know, he was 26 years old. And he's-- they will take him to the army. You wouldn't be able to, to resist another life like this, going through. Go where you can live a better life. Israel is beautiful, but it's hard life.

speaker: interviewer
text: So President Truman opened the gates for the survivors because before, you needed-- you needed a visa to go to America. And, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we registered. And we came to America. Tell me when you came.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came the end of December, you know, December 18--

speaker: interviewee
text: December 28--

speaker: interviewee
text: 1949. And we came to America. We were on-- you know, 10 days on the ship, and it wasn't the easy life. The story, what--

speaker: interviewer
text: With your kids you came?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course not with my youngest son. With my son, Solomon. He was he was three years old already. His-- we came on January-- his birthday was on January 5-- with the hope.

speaker: interviewer
text: The, the second son you have was born here?

speaker: interviewee
text: In America, was born in 1951 in Brooklyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me his name.

speaker: interviewee
text: His name is Joseph-- very bright. I'm very ha-- you know, I'm very proud of him. He is a professor at the University of Buffalo. My oldest son, Solomon, he finished the Pratt Institute in Chicago. He got his masters. He went for architecture. They're both bright. They also finished yeshiva. They're educated in Hebrew and in English-- I don't have to tell you. And I'm very proud of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're very devoted sons, and I'm the proudest mother to have my sons and to be together. I should only live to have nachas from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to stop.
